author: SOSYBOI
title: Hydrogen Bomb vs Coughing Baby
comment_content:
Reminder that this course in the future is not going to be as hard as this term. (Unless there is an even "crazier" professor than Amir which is very very unlikely.) This course was really something special. A lot of things have covered this term. It was fun definitely. I love math after all. But to master each topic in a week and train hard. That made my enjoyment throughout the course decrease day by day, week by week. The course this term has a lot more connection to higher level math courses (Real Analysis, Combinatorial Analysis, Game Theory, etc. just look at the syllabus and you will understand.)

Here is the syllabus and after that there will be the schedule for the whole course.

Syllabus:
    Proofs and Reasoning: Propositional Logic, First-order (Predicate) Logic, Quantifiers, Proof by contradiction, Mathematical Induction, the Pigeonhole Principle, the Invariant Principle, the Extremal Principle, ...
    Set Theory: ZMC, Peano's Axioms, Dedekind Cuts, Relations, Functions, Ordinals and Cardinals, Russel's Paradox, ...
    Enumerative Combinatorics: Rules of Sum and Product, Permutations and Combinations, Inclusion-Exclusion, Catalan and Stirling Numbers, Generating Functions, Recurrence Relations, Double Counting, ...
    Probability Theory: Discrete and Countable Probability Spaces, Axiomatic Probability Theory, Measure Functions, Lebesgue Integrals, Conditional Probability, Independence, Random Variables, Bayesian Reasoning, Expectation, Stochastic Processes, Markov Chains, Markov Decision Processes, ...
    Number Theory: Divisibility, GCD, LCM, Primes, Congruence, Chinese Remainder Theorem, Theorems of Fermat, Euler and Wilson, Quadratic Reciprocity Law, the RSA and El-Gamal Cryptosystems, ...
    Graph Theory: Adjacency, Walks, Paths, Cycles, (Strong) Connectivity, Distance, Trees, Spanning Trees, Bipartite Graphs, Matchings, Shortest Paths, ...
    Game Theory: Combinatorial Games, Sprague-Grundy Theorem, Normal Form (Nash) Games, Nash Equilibria, Correlated Equilibria, Infinite Games on Graphs, ...

Some of them are not covered by Amir, but you are required to read the books to learn it.

Here is how the flow so far according to how Amir teaches (From first lecture to latest lectures):

Logic and Proofs: Propositional Logic, Predicate, Quantifiers, definitions of natural numbers ,Peano's Axioms , Induction, Well-ording , Infinite Descent , introduction to Pigeonhole Principle, Strong Induction, Extremal Principle.

Combinatorics in general: Counting, Permutation, Combination with and without repetitions, Principle of Inclusion and Exclusion. More about Pigeonhole Principle.

Big focus on Graph Theory: theorems for graphs, isomorphism , path, walks, connected graphs, connected components, trees, cycles, algorithms on creating trees, bipartite, eulerian circuit, Hamlitonian cycle/graphs, graphical graphs , Havel Hakimi Algorithm ,Minimum Spanning Trees, Kruskal's algorithm, Prim's Algorithm , directed graph, DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph), Topological Ordering, DFS, BFS algorithms, Huffman Coding algorithm, eccentricity, shortest path, dijkstra algorithm, matchings, Hall's marriage theorem,König-Egeváry theorem, Gallai Theorem , vertex covers, edge covers, independent sets, network flow, ford-fulkerson algorithm, stable matching and graph coloring.

Number Theory: extension to Peano axiom to integers rather than natural number,definition of negative numbers, definiton of operations on integers, absolute value,divisibility, gcd, relatively prime/coprime, Euclidean algorithm, lcm, prime factorization, fundamental of arithmetic Congruence, Modular Multiplicative Inverse, Chinese Remainder Theorem, Fermat Little Theorem, Euler Theorem, Wilson's theorem, Diffie-Hellman-Merkle Key Exchange (Symmetric key), Elgamal encryption.

Set Theory: Naive Comprehension/Naive Set Theory, Russell's Paradox, Defining language for Set Theory, ZFC 10 Axioms (Extensionality, Empty Set, Unordered pair, Union, Comprehension, Powerset, Infinity, Replacement, Foundation, Axiom of Choice (there are 5 variations of this axiom)), Cardinality (e.g. Countable and Uncountable sets, Cantor's diagonalization, Taski's Theorem, Schroder-Berstein Theorem), Defining Rational and Real numbers (Dedekind cuts, supremum and infimum).

Probability Theory: Lebesgue Measure (Not heavily covered but it turns out to be very important in a hw), Measurable Space and Probability Space, Conditional Probability, Bayes Theorem, Independence, Random Variable, Linearity, Expectation, Stochastic Process and Markov Chain (Definition and representation in graphs especially having infinite walks in a graph), Reachability event, Buchi set, . (There are other topics Amir didn't taught in class like Variance and the distributions but they are required.)

Game Theory: Combinatorial Games (Nim with 2,3 and n heaps), solve games using Topological ordering in a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) and XOR operations, Sprague-Grundy Theorem, One shot games (Prisoner's Dilemma, Pollution game, Battle of sexes, Matching Pennies), Pure Nash Equilibrium (Using Dominant strategy to solve game), Mixed Nash Equilibrium (Using Probabilistic strategy to solve games), Nash Theorem, Arena, Reachability game (similar to Markov Chain), Buchi game.

There are also other topics that have connections to this course some are required some don't. (Control Theory, Group Theory, Measure Theory, Linear temporal logic, Ordinality of Set Theory, )

There are a lot of books to read. And most of them are not friendly to beginners at all. There are alternatives but these books goes deeper than any books I found in the library. Of course you go look at the "handbook of Discrete Math" but it doesn't really give you strong idea of why this or that theorem is true. So you still need to read the books.
I like Chen and Koh book.

Discrete and Combinatorial Mathematics by Grimaldi
Concrete Mathematics by Knuth et al
Principles and Techniques in Combinatorics by Chen and Koh
A First Course in Probability by Ross
Elementary Number Theory by Burton
Set Theory: A First Course by Cunningham
Introduction to Graph Theory by West
Algorithmic Game Theory by Nisan and Roughgarden
Problem Solving Strategies by Engel
102 Combinatorial Problems by Andreescu and Feng
Winning Ways for Your Mathematical Plays by Berlekamp et al

There are also some videos TAs or Amir might share but I recommend watching more that are not provided by them since a lot of the topics / questions really require guides or clarification. There are a lot of online resources related to this course so use them wisely! There is too many channels I can recommend and there are a lot of the topic this course has covered. So I recommend watch the videos in categories (Like watch videos about proofs like MIT courses, organic chemistry(Probably the weakest channel with proofs but very basic), TrevTutor, Math Sorcerer.)

If you have read this far, here is a reward to you. His website to his course with the recordings. https://amir.goharshady.com/teaching/honors-discrete-math
comment_teaching:
Amir is a good and fun teacher. However, the pacing is really fast. And don't expect the notes by Amir and TAs to be detailed. (He is pretty lazy when it comes to writing notes but he wrote so much in problem sets/homework/exam questions idk why lol.) TAs simply just copy Amir notes and take some words from him in the recording. So, make sure to take notes as well. I had to rewatch lecture notes and read chapters on books multiple times to get a grasp on a single content. Also for those who don't know, all tutorials sections are just additional lectures for students to attend and they are mandatory. So if you miss one lecture, you are in a disadvantage unless you have a very good background on Discrete Math before hand. I highly recommend you guys to ask TAs and Amir for help. They are friendly. But make sure to book the meetings asap.

TAs are good. I had problems from listening to one of the TAs since he don't speak English well. But I still think he is a decent TA. Since he patiently answers your questions. The TA tutor sections are also very crucial for the course. So attend them as well. If not watch the recording.
comment_grading:
Homework 40%
Mid Term Exam 30%
Final Exam 30%

The homework policy is quite generous in general, you are allowed to submit twices for each homework. After the first submission, you get feedback from TAs or Amir and then resubmit it. The score for each homework is the maximum of two attempts.

As for the homework difficulty, it is generally hard after homework 1.
HW1: 10/10
HW2: 8.6/10
HW3: 10/10
HW4: 10/10

HW4 is where you need to understand Measure Theory and there is literally nothing you can do except googling.

As for the mid term, well it was pretty devastating. Even though it is easier than weekly problem sets, it is generally hard. The mid term covered EVERYTHING before the the date of the mid term exam, that means the lectures for that week also counts into it. Also, the weekly problem sets solutions are quite late to come out. I understand that TAs are very busy but I wish at least there are guides on how to do these type of questions OR just give us a sample paper like they did in 3711H.

As for my score, I got 6/30, my friend had 8/30. (Yes, I am terrible. I am sure most of the students here have higher score than me. I had mental breakdown before I had the grade and asked TA for help to save me from failing the course.)

As for the final exam, while it is easier, it turns out to have question on calculating which is unexpected. I was expecting something similar to the problem sets. So I didn't score perfectly on that part. Same goes for Combinatorics. Other than that, it was not that bad. I got 16.5/30. Much much better than mid term.

I got A- in the end. THANK YOU AMIR YOU ARE THE BEST!!!
comment_workload:
This course has ridiculous workload. Not only you have lectures that you cannot skip. You have extra exercises that are required to do. Especially counting problems. In week 2, Amir have given us over 400 problems and some of them are advanced questions involving group theory. This is impossible to do and I only managed to do 1/4 of it from sacrificing my daily time and skipping lectures.

There are totally 12 problem sets with 10 problems each. They are not required but very highly recommended. The problem set are usually hard, there are easy ones. But when it comes to counting/principle of inclusion and exclusion/pigeonhole principle/number theory, Amir loves to add Olympiad questions to it and they are generally very hard. I believe the TAs just copy the solutions in the internet as their solutions. (And how they find the observation to solution is really unclear.). So expect to spent a long time to finish these questions as well. I didn't do problem sets after set 7 since I fumbled other courses while mainly handling this course.

The homework at the beginning is pretty easy as long as you do some combinatorics questions. After that it is not easy. So you might spent a few days if not weeks to solve 2-3 questions. Btw, student can only submit their hw via latex, so if you have no knowledge on it, have fun spending extra time on it. (I stared at the screen for days. Always thinking the solutions while eating, walking, laying on bed or taking a bath. It took me a long time to get them right.) Don't forget, there are questions that you need to learn how to answer in the internet as Amir didn't taught them much. You need to give correctness on your algorithm, proving it is correct. Read/watch Measure Theory and Markov Decision Process.

There are many books you can read but they are high recommended again (The books are in the contents part since they are pretty much compulsory). But Amir also shares videos or extra reading materials. The videos are usually long but you can skip them if you want to. I watched and read all of them.

Oh yes, we cannot forget about the study part even though we did a lot already. There are a few ways you can study. Either you watch videos relate to it or watch other courses taught by Amir. It is very easy to see the similaries and there are exam papers publish publicly there. (No idea why no sample papers for this course. smh).

For those who dislike the tremendous workload like this, don't take this course. Although Amir is no longer teaching in UST, he is still a great teacher. He just likes to make things 100x harder.

author: Tedddddddddddy
title: 很好的老师
comment_teaching:
很幽默很清晰，个人觉得虽然有回放，但是现场听效果会好很难多，因为非常需要集中精力
但是Amir走了 :(
comment_grading: 我做得并不是很好，因为期末时间花在了别的科目，但是只要跟着听基本就有A range
comment_workload:
你说的对，但是2711H是一门由Amir自主开发的全新开放思维冒险课程。课程开设在一个被称作HKUST的幻想世界，在这里，人们将被要求引导算法之力，完成小学奥数。你将扮演一位名为「Computer Scientist」的神秘角色，在自由的学习中邂逅不会计算、但一起绽放笑容的同伴们，和他们一起找回年少自信。（听说学2711H的同学都天生爱笑：）
毫不夸张地说，2711H是一门为大一新生量身定做，被大二学长强力推荐，零门槛，难度适中，给龟easy，考试人性化的优秀课程。Amir随性幽默专业的谈吐，更让学习变成一种享受。经常上2711H的同学大多眼神锐利，极度自信，且智力逐渐增加。上2711H会重组大脑结构，让小脑变为大脑，最后完全变成「Computer Scientist」。上2711H的学生具有强烈的数感，人一旦开始爱上2711H，就说明这个人gpa通通都是上等，这辈子肯定在UST大杀四方，对世界格局起到决定性的作用。
一个不喜欢2711H的人，无非只能有两种可能性。一种是不认为省队竞赛题、IMO试题与小猿口算难度相当的人。因为自己没有能力抽出一点时间去读每周几本书的课余小任务等自身因素，而把做不出题归咎于题目难度，他们的人生是失败的。如果你学三周2711H却不能拿信竞金牌，只能说明你没有思考足够长时间的week problem set；如果你学三周2711H没能参加IMOCMOUSAMO并拔得头筹，只能说明你对Amir的recommend book list视而不见。第二种可能：没有感受到Amir个人魅力的人。那么这个人的思想境界便低到了一个令人发指的程度。一个人不喜欢Amir，只能证明此人个人品味之低下，是阴暗的，是扭曲的，是无法融入社会的。Amir怎么你了，我每天18小时观看Amir课程回放，每节课能学到2到3年世界前列的数学研究成果。我半节课学完逻辑门，1天能熟练使用latex，2节课掌握数归良序递降，3周精通图论数论，我一门语言都不会，却能轻松理解DFS、BFS、动态规划以及各种NP完全问题，虽然我只读大一，但已经超越了中国绝大多数人（包括你）的水平，换算过来最少也是Yau的徒弟了，这便是我骄傲的资本。这恰好说明了2711H课程使学生体现出来的谦虚和自信，这也造就了这个课程学生互帮互助的局面。Amir负责课上输出优质内容，学生负责课后形成良好的学习氛围，如此良好的课程模式令那些绞尽脑汁却找不到人听课的教授艳羡不已，令lang课group discussion只能听到2711H讨论声的老师自惭形秽。可以说，选课不选2711H，就像四大名著不读红楼梦，说明对于数学造诣和自我修养不足，理解不了算法与哲学统一魅力的高雅艺术，它只能看到外表的数字和计算，参不透计算机异或内核，只能度过一个相对失败的人生。

author: GPA爆4
title: Bye Amir
comment_content:
If you are trying to read this review to get a hang of how the course is like and whether you should take it, I suggest you leave. And do not read any of the other reviews on fall 2024-25, because Amir has already left ust and you will not be able to take this version of 2711H ever again.

Moving on to the content part, please read the other reviews of the same semester to get a hang of what was taught. I don't know if any of you have ever came in contact with olympiad mathematics. Questions you see in this course is extremely similar to them. I have came in contact with them when I was in primary school so I think I handled it better than some people in my class.
comment_teaching:
Amir is a walking aura emitter. His teaching is one of its kind. I have never seen anyone in my life teach at the same level as him. He has the ability to explain a difficult concept in a few minutes with extremely precise language. He is like Desmond, but he teaches at 3 times his speed. How he does it is by presenting his logic flow in an ordered manner, splitting each problem into smaller problems, and solving each sub problems to prove the actual question.

I also like his approach of teaching. He basically just gives us problems in lessons, explains how to do it, and move on to the next problem. This way, the content is easier to understand and you also have the opportunity to think on your own before seeing the answer.

I also feel like what I learned from him is more than knowledge. It's a mindset. It's an approach to difficult discrete mathematics problems. Whenever I struggle in some problems, I try to think what Amir would do. I would rewatch his videos and learn the way he proves. At some point, I just realised the method of proofing became part of my toolbox. If the hammer doesn't work, I will try using the screwdriver. I will keep testing until it works, and it really sped up my problem solving process as I no longer have to sit in front of the problem and wonder how I am suppose to solve it.

By the way, please check out his youtube channel if you want to have a taste of what his teaching is like or want to pre study 2711H. It's very challenging but worth watching. I think the only part he didn't teach well was number theory. The rest of them were all very good.
comment_grading:
40% homework
30% midterm
30% final
my mark:
hw1 10/10
hw2 8.8/10
hw3 10/10
hw4 7/10
midterm 9.5/30
final 15/30
final grade: A-
I don't actually know if this course has good grading or not because Amir said that he was not allowed to disclose the statistics. But from what I know from previous semesters and the fact that I barely studied for final exam, I am just gonna guess that it has good grading as I still got A range.
comment_workload:
The workload is supposed to be E. The reason I gave it an A is because I didn't want to mess up the overall rating of the course too badly as I know this version of 2711H is not gonna appear anymore.

As the other reviews had mentioned, there are 10 books amir told us to read, additional videos each week to watch and problem sets every week. The workload is not for humans. I could study this course alone in the whole semester and I wouldn't be able to finish all the work he gave us. So I basically gave up on doing any of them after the fifth week and hope that everyone else is doing the same. I think Amir gave us so much exercise and resources because that's how he studied when he was a student? I am just guessing though. Anyway Amir had high expectations of us and we failed to meet it.

comment_grading: Everyone has two chances for each homework, most students will achieve homework/exam marks that are lower than what they are used to in other courses.

author: Think
title: Just take this
comment_content: Same as 2711. Maybe a little bit deeper, but basically the same.
comment_teaching: He is a great teacher. Shows everything step by step and very clearly so that everyone can understand. The lectures are quite different from the notes on the course website, but in the essence, they are the same. Still, it is very recommended to attend the lecture since you can gain much deeper understanding of the concept much easier.
comment_grading:
Grading is great. More than half gets A range. The scheme was

MT 35%
Final 45%
Assignments 20%

The exams have high means, yet if you study the notes, examples in class, past papers, and especially the Question Bank and Extra exercises carefully, you will be able to get high marks.
comment_workload: All you have to do is just study for the exam plus just 4 assignments. The assignments tend to be quite hard and mean is very high either: still, if you search online, you have access to most of the answers, and even the lecturer do not stop you from doing so. He thinks it is one of the ways you learn.

author: dasnaq
title: How is this easier than comp2711
comment_content:
Basically the same with comp2711, only difference being the order of content

Lectures 1-3: Logic
- Propositional logic
- Predicate logic
- Rules of inference, proofs

(Self-study: sets and functions)
(No countable and uncountable stuff unlike comp2711)

Lectures 4-7: Number theory and algorithms
- Modular arithmetic
- GCDs and congruences
- RSA cryptography

Lectures 8-11: Counting
- Sum rule, product rule, pigeonhole principle
- Permutations and combinations
- Binomial coefficients
- Inclusion-exclusion principle

Lectures 12-14: Probability
- Intro to probability
- Conditional probability
- Random variables

Lectures 15-16: Induction and recursion
- Induction
- Recursion

Lectures 17: Graphs (may cut depending on teaching progress)
comment_teaching:
Arya is the reason I come to ust instead of skipping lectures because he teaches concepts super clearly, so I don't have to read the slides again to understand the content. He is also very enthusiastic and passionate, and his lessons are quite engaging. The best part about him is that he tries to present proofs insightfully. For example, he uses Euler's totient function to show how someone can come up with RSA cryptography instead of just restating the ppt proof.

Tbh tho if you're a fast learner or have previously learnt the materials in primary school then Arya's style might not suit you because his pace is quite slow for an honors course. He also teaches with intuition in mind so you might feel like he is treating you like a kid if you're an analytical guy.
comment_grading:
Somehow the papers are easier than comp2711 in the same semester, but the grading can be strict as they require you to justify almost everything. In fact, they can even give you full marks if your logic is correct but you made careless mistakes midway. However, if you forgot to explain how you got to a certain equation or inequality (e.g., writing down 1+(n/(n+1))<=2 without explanation), then they will deduct marks.

Grading scheme:
20% Assignment
35% Midterm
45% Final

Midterm:
- Min / LQ / Mean / Median / UQ / Max: 32 / 69 / 80.71 / 85 / 97 / 100
- SD: 17.20
- Mode: 100 (10 people achieved this score)
(Misc: Skewness: -0.72, Kurtosis (Excess): -0.41)

Final:
- Min / LQ / Mean / Median / UQ / Max: 3 / 65.5 / 75.68 / 80 / 90.5 / 100
- SD (before paper checking): 20.68

My scores:
HW total: 18.25/20
Midterm: 98/100
Final: 98/100
Total score: 96.65%
Letter grade: A+

Attached are the midterm and final papers. (yes arya allows us to take question papers back home)
comment_workload:
Just 4 easy assignments every 2 weeks cus if you're going into honors these should be a walk in the park.

Just kidding assignments are difficult but they are still kind of doable. If you have no clue how to do it feel free to consult AI for help, but it's better to do it yourself because once you see the solution it's really obvious. You'll still do fine if you use AI though because Arya's midterm and final questions don't require many "tricks" to solve.

Apart from that it's just the workload from preparing midterms and finals.

author: xdlok
title: Don't be afraid of the 2000 title! Take it if you meet the prerequisites!
comment_content:
MATH course with COMP course code. The content is similar to the non honor version:
Basic logic, proofs; combinatorics, discrete probability; Number theory, cryptography; Induction, recursion, Big O, omega, theta notations; Graphs.
The prerequisites are not really needed because no calculus are required, but good problem skills are (good DSE core combinatorics and probability knowledge would be a great plus).
The content is appropriate even for a year 1 student like myself.
comment_teaching:
HIs teaching is really good and the content is good. He encourges thinking about a topic or question deeper because this is honor class. He is funny as well. Although he has an accent, it is comprehensible.
The ta is just ok, we only do a few questions in the tutorial, looked at the answer for a little while, and move on.
comment_grading:
The midterm have a mean of 78 and a sd of about 15, and the exam have a mean of 81. I got slightly above mean for both, but below mean for all homework, but still got an A.

It is worth mentioning that the midterm is heavily "inspired" by the homework, as prof pointed out. I was shock though, seeing the same question about proving the Chebeyshev Inequality (worth 10 points), and overall are quite similar to the homework. The exams are just a memorization fest where you write out things you memorized from the notes. Just read all the notes and you are done.
comment_workload: OH BOY DOES HE MEAN IT WHEN HE SAID THAT THE WORKLOAD IS HIGH. There are 4 homeworks, with about 9 - 14 days to finish the homework, which consists of about 10 - 15 question. Those questions are very hard, really require you to spend a whole night to think about, doesn't work, and try another way. I ended up googling some of them. And only some of the questions were graded each time, and they chose the easy one. If you did well on the questions that they didn't mark, well, bad luck for you.

author: ayymax
comment_content: Based on what I know, there is so many topics discussed in this semester's COMP 2711H, especially when compared with previous courses. With Amir, this might as well be a 5-6 credit course considering how much he expands on in every topic. We have everything in the normal COMP 2711 and also a bunch of measure theory, graph theory, game theory, and a bunch of others.
comment_teaching: I think Amir is fantastic in teaching, he even has his youtube for every lecture he has taught in UST (Farewell Amir, he is leaving to Oxford), you can get his lectures on his youtube channel, might be good to prepare for COMP 2711 or 2711H.
comment_grading:
40% homework, 30% MT, 30% finals.
At the time of this review, the course final has not been done yet so I can't really say on how good the grading is. Supposedly most people should get B or A on this course. Homework is doable, Amir gives resubmissions and comments for homework. However considering how wide the topics for this course is, I think it is pretty hard to have a good grasp on every topic, so it will be harder to score on MT and finals.
comment_workload: Overall, the workload is very unreal, Amir gives us reccomendation to read about 10 books, and I think that you might have trouble following up on every single one of them, unless you are only focusing on this one course. He also gives a problem set every week to practice on and 4 homework, but other than that, theres not much to work on. The sheer amount of books and topics covered will demotivate a normal person very quickly, so I think its best to do it one at a time.

author: Kokomi
title: 好课值得上（大一上可选）
comment_content:
Not hard but interesting. If you are a mainland student, you may find the content quite easy. Taking this course in year 1 fall is definitely okay because this course actually does not require any MATH1014 or programming knowledge. All you need is a waiver XD.

# Content list #
Propositional logic & Predicate logic & Inference
(命题的概念，比较基础)
Mod & GCD & Crypto
(整除、最大公约数、加密解密。个人觉得RSA加密的部分比较有趣，也算是一个略难的知识点)
Counting & Permutations and Combinations & Binomial Coefficients & Inclusion and Exclusion
(计数原理、排列组合、二项式系数、容斥定理。小学奥数+高中数学内容)
Algorithms
(算法分析、函数的增长。这部分就是新内容了，据说后面学算法的话也会比较有用)
Probability & Conditional probability & Expectation and Variance
(概率、条件概率、期望和方差。高中数学内容)
Induction & Recursion
(数学归纳法、递归。前者高中内容，后者对有写码经验的同学来说应该也不陌生)
Graph
(这学期因为台风+暴雨冲了2节lecture的样子，最后这一节没讲，只让我们自己看了讲义，当然考试也没考)
comment_teaching:
Prof. Arya is passionate in teaching and is willing to answer questions. He always replies to emails quickly. If you can get used to his Indian accent (I did that in 2 weeks), then the teaching would be perfect.
By contrast, tutorial makes me sleepy and the TAs are not that good. Luckily, the content in tutorials are not difficult and they don't take attendance, so skipping the tutorials is not a bad idea.
comment_grading:
# grading policy #
homework 10% + midterm 40% + final 50%

# statistics #
hw1: low-mean-high = 13.0-19.0-20.0 || mine = 20/20
hw2: low-mean-high = 12.0-23.8-25.0 || mine = 25/25
midterm : low-mean-high = 32.0-86.5-100.0 || mine = 100/100
hw3: low-mean-high = 00.0-22.7-25.0 || mine = 21/25
hw4: low-mean-high = 13.0-19.2-20.0 || mine = 19/20
final : low-mean-high = 15.0-65.8-100.0 || mine = 100/100
overall: mine = 99.475 -> A+
Quite competitive but good grading. 挺卷的，不过好龟

The exams are actually much easier than COMP2711. Many problems test your understanding of the lectures. Many people got full in midterm. Final is kind of harder, but it’s not too difficult to solve all the problems because some problems even include hints. The problems are beautiful and well-designed, which means that grading is quite fair. It seems that the exam time is relatively long (3 hours for 10 problems). Making sure you understand everything in lectures, homework and past papers is very helpful for exams.
comment_workload: 4 homeworks in total, 2~3h each. You are required to finish each of them in about 2 weeks. Besides, it takes some time to fully understand some difficult content (only in several lectures). Generally speaking, the workload is light for a 4-credit course.

author: LKR
comment_teaching:
I really like the way Amir teaches. His lectures start with understanding a problem and then go on to solving it, so that knowledge can be presented in a form suitable for entering the brain. He gave almost every theorem understandable proof, and the matching examples are sufficient, multi-angle and not excessive.
So I enjoyed his class.

As for the formal assignments, they should be handed in latex, which I think is good practice of latex ability.
I also like the process of doing homework. Difficult questions can get students to discuss spontaneously.
A wrong question got me thinking for a long time, but it wasn't that necessary.

At first I was still working on the Problem Sets, and then I stopped working on it. I fell behind about 5, but I almost made up for the final week.
TA’s lectures are not as good as Amir, but at least their academic ability is good. Later, I found that the answers they wrote were also quite convenient to read.

Exams are fun. It brings together the content of the lecture in a way I never thought possible.

Through this course, I found that learning professional knowledge can be as interesting as listening to talk shows and playing games to fight monsters. Thanks Amir for not only bringing me knowledge, but also bringing me interest.
comment_grading: The grading is good.
comment_workload: I think wl is flexible. Those who want to learn more can do homework and read more textbooks.

author: Sausage0819
title: The math is different from the others, it is difficult for some (including me)
comment_content: The course teaches you to think in different ways in order to solve math problems. And sometimes, the solutions are really unexpected. No matter how great you were in calc or other math-related subjects, it means nothing here. The other subjects have some clear methodology for different kinds of problems, but you won’t find it here. For example, the solution in a function problem is to count boxes.
comment_teaching: Arya was passionate in teaching, and is alway willing to explain the concept, provide insights and tips to students. The TAs are just average, their tutorials are kinda boring but are useful for strengthening your understanding
comment_grading:
This is the part where you may die, the mean is high af. Yes, the exams are easy but if discrete math is something new to you, you’re fucked.
Midterm mean: 86
Me in midterm: 73
It was partly because I got my head stuck at the end, and also due to my bad habit in factorising whatever tf is in the question. But I think the problem is that some of us lack exposure to these kinds of math in highschools. Btw I got in here because I got 5* in M2 so I’m Y1, maybe that’s also 1 of the reason

Final is unknown yet but I’m trying my best to make it above mean so I can still at least have an A-

Update after a whole year lol:
The grading is objectively great, I got below mean by ~20 scores which is 53 or sth back in the final.
Guess what? Got a B, this is the most leng grade course I've studied in UST so far. But beware, your course-mates are also competitive af. GG (in Desmond's tone)
comment_workload: Will be large if you’re like me, very new to these types of subjects, you will need more experience of doing questions such that you can think of a solution in the exams. The homework and supplementary questions will help, but there are always 2 to 3 questions in the homework that is hard af, you will probably search for a solution online or you’ll lose your hair thinking

author: qjk
title: Not as good as the other reviews say
comment_content: Basically the same content as in COMP2711. The order of teaching is different, however. Also, is this semester graphs were covered in H but not in non-H.
comment_teaching: Sunil is a good teacher. He has a bit of an accent but you can still clearly understand what he is saying. Both Sunil and the TAs are helpful and will respond to emails promptly. I sent many emails to TA Jiachuan before the finals and he responded in detail to all my questions. One time he even responded within 4 minutes. The whole teaching team is very professional.
comment_grading:
HWs 10% at 2.5% each
Midterm 40%
Finals 50%

Me:
HW: 20/20 20/20 18/20 20/20
Midterm 95/100 - Mean 87.5 SD ~13
Finals 92/100 - Stats not official released, mean should be 89.9 according to canvas
Total course grade: 93.75%
Grade: A

In the midterm I lost 5 marks because I forgot the whole proof of Euler's GCD algorithm, and in the finals I lost 8 marks because I made a careless mistake.

For comparison, I hear a 91.75% in 2711 was worth an A+ in the semester I took 2711H. So I basically signed up for a shit load of extra work with no extra gain GPA-wise. I spent 7 (seven) days revising for the finals, man.

I don't know what other ppl got, but FYI Sunil said the mean would be around A-.
comment_workload:
The workload is intense.

The HW is insane and I needed to google all the answers, but it is okay if you google the answers but you memorize how the questions are done.

The question sets are optional but you need to do them to get an A. There are questions sets and supplementary question sets and all of them are useful. Half the exam questions are copy pasted from the HW and the question sets.

For the finals, I spent 7 days revising for it, going through all the questions sets and HWs and doing some past papers. Sunil will release the past papers.

author: CyrusL
comment_content:
Similar content as that of COMP 2711.

1. Logic and Proof
2. Modular Arithmetic
3. Combinatorics
---- MIDTERM EXAM ----
4. Probability and Statistics
5. Induction and Recursion
6. Graphs*
---- FINAL EXAM ----

*Graphs will only be covered if time allows. In this semester, the professor finished around half of it.
comment_teaching:
The professor explains the concepts in much detail (as expected for a honors course) and often emphasizes the importance of thinking about the same problem in different ways, so as to build a richer mathematical sense. Possibly as a result, the proofs in class may differ from the ones displayed in the lecture notes posted on the course website. General recommendation is to not skip class, although the professor said himself that it is totally alright to just read the lecture notes. If you do attend class, be warned that using mobile phones is frowned upon (laptops are fine).

On the other hand, the tutorials are just about average. What happens is that several problems are selected from the question bank (which will be posted on the course website beforehand) to be explained by the TA, which is, frankly, quite boring. If you have done all of the questions in advance and checked your answers, the tutorials can be skipped. Only attend if you have questions.
comment_grading:
Assignments 20%
Midterm 35%
Final Exam 45%

For each assignment, only 4 to 5 questions are chosen randomly to be graded.

Both the midterm and final exam cover all content taught previously. Many of the questions were taken directly or closely adapted from the question bank, so be sure to use the question bank as exam practice! The final exam was significantly easier than the midterm for this semester.

The professor mentioned that as long as you put in the same amount of effort, the grade achieved should be around the same as if you took COMP 2711. Having a mean score should guarantee you an A-.

MY RESULTS
Assignments: 93.5/100 (Mean: 92.8, S.D.: 8.1)
Midterm: 53/100 (Mean: 70.0, S.D.: 20.3)
Final Exam: 97/100 (Mean: 81.0, S.D.: 19.8)
Overall: 80.9/100 (Mean: 79.5, S.D.: 15.9)
Rank: 38/60
Grade: A-

The professor released the scores of each and every student (identified by last few digits of SID); couldn't hope for more transparency than that.
comment_workload:
There are 4 assignments, each with around 10 questions. Depending on the difficulty, each assignment can take anywhere from 4 to 16 hours to complete (assuming that you do not search online for answers). Both handwritten and printed work are accepted (hard copies only).

Considering that I also took COMP 2012H during this semester, the workload for this course isn't really that much... right?

If you are not interested in discrete mathematics, taking COMP 2711 may be a better idea.

author: Noker
title: The only class I actually go to in this semester
comment_content: Less than high school math Olympiad number theory and combinatorics , but have some intro to crypto, induction, recursion, algorithm, graph theory, quite light for people with some proof experience, as a good bridge for real analysis in terms of building up math maturity for math majors as well. Basically the same as 2711 but we have a much better professor here (his UST ranking is top 5)
comment_teaching:
Prof Arya’s teaching is great, really passionate and cares about math beauty (especially in combinatorial proofs), despite previewing ppt before each lecture, he always manages to say something I never thought of, and he tells a lot of jokes, (one time accidentally say “what the f…” and took it back hahahah) Also, he and the TAs reply to emails fast.

Update: His teaching gets better as the stuff gets harder. After mid terms, we started combinatorics and probability. I actually learnt the probability properly in here but not in ISOM 2500 (business statistics) which I learnt nothing there. Also, he asks us to first make a guess, and then do the math rigorously so ensure that we are thinking on the right track, this mindset is really good and makes us do maths more mindfully, really really appreciate his teaching!!

Update (towards the end of sem): Prof Arya didn’t manage to finish all of graph theory. As set theory was excluded as well (but the concepts related to injection and surjection are included in combinatorics), we can see that 2711H covers less materials than 2711, but has more in depth analysis of other key topics.

(Update): on the last day of the course he asked if the materials are too difficult, but then he said but it’s too late to drop the course. What a good way to end the course. Hahaha
comment_grading:
Mid term:
Mean: 80.71, sd: 17.2, median: 85, UQ: 97, mode: 100, yes, 10 people out of 90 got 100 marks. It’s insane, I shouldn’t be here.
The grading of questions are really really lenient, try to write some definitions out even if you have entirely no clue. I was one step away from finishing the last question, but they regarded my answer to be totally correct.

Before finals, Prof Arya announced that the final would be super hard as we performed too well in mid terms (he wanted the mean to be 70 but we got 80, so he would make the final mean at around 55-60). Actually quite scared, let’s see what happens.

Final exam: mean 75.68, sd 20.68, median 80, UQ 90.5.
However, despite Prof Arya saying it would be difficult, the final exam was very easy, I’d say much easier than hw, I struggled a lot with the assignments, and after struggling, you’d find the exam paper very easy. However, please make sure to understand the tricks from the assignment as they will appear exactly in your final, and he even have us doing it twice in the last 2 Qs. This year, 2711H paper is way easier than that of 2711. There’s really no reason not to take this.

2711 is 2.5h for 11Q and 2711H is 3h for 8Q. Also, we were not tested on materials before mt at all but 2711 did. We didn’t have graph at all but 2711 did. So now you understand the difficulty of 2711H, plus 50% A range, is there any reason not to take this?

My score:
Assignment (20%): 20/20 (mean is around 18)
Mid term (35%): 97/100 (I forgot the proof of gcd)
Final (45%): 100/100 (really easy if you understand all pp and hw)
Total: 98.95%
Grade: A+ (I definitely cannot get A+ in regular 2711)

Please note that they care a lot about how you present your proofs/steps. Even for non-proof questions, it’s the safest to write down all the steps and formulae with justification, and explain how you got each step to the next step with a very short sentence or a bracket next to the steps to indicate some properties. As for algebraic steps, they deduct marks if you skip a lot of them as they think you may not know how things cancel out or combine, you just see the final answer (proof questions) and pretend you did it
comment_workload:
2 assignments before mt and 2 before finals, released 14 days before submission and you know exactly when they will be released in the course syllabus.
Before mt, as I have some prior knowledge, I usually finish the assignments within 2 days after releasing, so the workload is light for me, but things are getting much harder after mid term, so wish me luck.

(Update): hw 3,4 are wild. I have no idea about half of the questions and I’m so cooked. It took me 5 days to finish each of them, and the solution is amazingly simple and elegant. (Btw, it is usually the case that you can finish one problem with half a page, if you’re writing pages of calculation,  you’re definitely on the wrong track, or at least, not the optimal track) Also, the assignments perfectly complement the materials from the lecture and extend it a little bit, I can feel the professors passion about utilising every opportunity to help us learn more and deeper.

author: LiShirui
comment_content:
Proofs and Reasoning: Propositional Logic, First-order (Predicate) Logic, Quantifiers, Proof by contradiction, Mathematical Induction, the Pigeonhole Principle, the Invariant Principle, the Extremal Principle, ...
Set Theory: ZMC, Peano's Axioms, Dedekind Cuts, Relations, Functions, Ordinals and Cardinals, Russel's Paradox, ...
Enumerative Combinatorics: Rules of Sum and Product, Permutations and Combinations, Inclusion-Exclusion, Catalan and Stirling Numbers, Generating Functions, Recurrence Relations, Double Counting, ...
Probability Theory: Discrete and Countable Probability Spaces, Axiomatic Probability Theory, Measure Functions, Lebesgue Integrals, Conditional Probability, Independence, Random Variables, Bayesian Reasoning, Expectation, Stochastic Processes, Markov Chains, Markov Decision Processes, ...
Number Theory: Divisibility, GCD, LCM, Primes, Congruence, Chinese Remainder Theorem, Theorems of Fermat, Euler and Wilson, Quadratic Reciprocity Law, the RSA and El-Gamal Cryptosystems, ...
Graph Theory: Adjacency, Walks, Paths, Cycles, (Strong) Connectivity, Distance, Trees, Spanning Trees, Bipartite Graphs, Matchings, Shortest Paths, ...
Game Theory: Combinatorial Games, Sprague-Grundy Theorem, Normal Form (Nash) Games, Nash Equilibria, Correlated Equilibria, Infinite Games on Graphs, ...

author: UnconMarketeers
title: Essential math course teaching essential math tools
comment_content:
[Some words after watching Amir's offering]

Apparently Amir's version is way more all-rounded than the usual offering, in terms of the depth and breadth of course content. In the typical version, the teaching sequence is

Propositional logic -> Number theory -> Combinatorics -> Probability Theory -> (Optional) Graph theory

But Amir's version has the sequence of

Propositional logic + Induction (a part of Peano's axiom) -> Combinatorics -> Graph theory + Graph algos -> Number theory + Cryptography -> Axiom of completeness -> Probability -> Stochastic process -> Game theory

If 3711H is "a feast for algo geeks", then 2711H shall be "a fine-dining for algebraists, analysts and algo geeks" since the non-graph-theoretic topics are really foundations for mathematicians living on each side, while the graph theory topics can be treated as a taster of 3711H since the related core topics are covered with some proof of correctness, but without the important C++ implementations and runtime analysis.

Initially I didn't understand why graph theory was introduced at the beginning of the course. After covering topics like Markov chains and game theory, I realized that there might be no better teaching sequences for putting graph theory elsewhere.

If you wish to go further based on the course content, the following courses are highly recommended -

(i). The Honors analysis sequence (1023 up to 3043) will help you continue completing the "axiom of completeness", as well as why measure theory and Lebesgue integration theory are necessary in modern analysis. I would say solely based on 2711H, students may feel difficult to see why Lebesgue integral is important and necessary.

(ii) The discussion on elementary number theory is just telling one fundamental fact that "\mathbb Z" is a Unique Factorization Domain (UFD)". The Honors (and graduate-level) algebra courses (3131 + 5111) will explore more algebraic structures and similar properties; Also many modern crypto algos are based on commutative algebra and algebraic geometry.

(iii) The measure-theoretic probability (2431 + 5411) courses will make use of what you learnt in the Honors analysis courses and rebuild every concepts in classical probability. Without such foundations, the famous central limit theorem and the laws of large numbers (and related variants) cannot be understood rigorously.

(iv) For the graph-theoretical topics, indeed 3711H is the best continuation.

Like 3711H, I admire the hard efforts made by Amir and his teaching team in this course.

[End]

1. Official textbook: Notes on the course website, and his own lecture - The content taught in class might be different from that in the notes, and not all stuff is taught.

2. Course structure:

========Part One: Pure Logic stuff=======

TL;DR: Literally basic building block in math.

i. Propositional logic, inference,... (Most useful takeaway: p implies q iff not q implies not p - A logical mistake that everyone makes in daily life)
ii. Set and cardinality
iii. Function (Mapping between sets), and injective, surjective, bijective...

==========Part Two: Number Theoty===========

TL;DR: Basic number theory.

iv. Mod arithmetic and number theory (Euclid's algorithm, Fermat's little theorem, C.R.T... - One shall study abstract algebra topics like group and ring for further study)
v. Cryptography (RSA)

========Part Three: Counting principle==========

TL;DR: One of the hardest types of question appears here! You have to prove identity, usually combinatorial approach instead of analytic. It is quite difficult because you may not figure out the correct scenario for the proof!

vi. Permutation and combination, binomial coefficient
vii. Inclusion-exclusion principle
viii. Analysis of algorithm (Big-Oh, Big-Theta, Big-Omega...)

=========Part Four: Probability======

TL;DR: Discrete random variables. The only continuous stuff taught is normal distribution.

ix. Random variable
x. Expectation and variance, conditional probability
xi. Special distribution (Bernoulli, binomial, geometric, normal...)

=========Part Five: Others=======

xii. Strong/weak induction
xiii. Recursion
xiv. Graph theory
comment_teaching: Interactive, encourage students to ask and answer questions. Although I never attend his lectures after midterm because the content in latter part is quite familiar for me, I still agree that the instructor is a good teacher.

author: nikita8125
title: A good course
comment_content:
The content of the course:

Predicate Logic
Rules of Inference
Modular Arithmetic
GCDs
Modular Multiplicative Inverses
Chinese Remainder Theorem
Cryptography
Asymptotic Notations
Probabilities
Binomial Coefficients, Polynomials and Combinatorics
Random Variables, Expectations: Definitions and Properties, Applications
Variance;
Inducion and Recursion
Graphs (were covered partially)

The set of topics is good and appropriate for those students who do not have a solid background in the field of computer science. Otherwise, one may find the content oversimplified and scarce. The content of lectures is stuck to the Rosen's textbook. Tutorials are helpful since the tasks provided are similar to those on the examinations.
comment_teaching:
Prof. Arya is very kind and understanding. In addition, the lectures are well-designed for those who find even basic proofs scary or tedious.
TAs provide a wide range of the tasks and their solutions that helps to grasp the foundations well.

If you are not a CS student, but want to pursue a major in DSCT, this course fits you well. Otherwise, you may have another opinion about the course.
comment_grading:
Grading scheme for the term:

20% Assignments
35% Midterm
45% Final

Examinations include both proof-based questions and calculations regarding the topics mentioned above. Proof-based questions are not complicated as long as you are familiar with the content and structure your proofs in a precise and appropriate manner. Students should review all the tutorials, past papers and PPTs before the exam - and it will be fine. In addition, homeworks are designed for the preparation to the exams since the basic solution techniques are the same for both HW and exams.

Despite the description in the syllabus, letter grades are assigned according to the curve. The grading policy is a trade-off. On the one hand, A-range is approximately 55-60%. On the other hand, means and medians for the course are high (roughly about 80 and 85, respectively).

Therefore, if you do not have any profound understanding of the concepts before the course, you might well get A- if you put in some effort and B+ without too much effort because B+ range is also wide. However, the competition for solid A and A+ is enormously fierce since the vast majority of the students are those who are familiar with the concepts well like informatics or math Olympiad participants, even IOI/IMO.
comment_workload:
Workload is quite heavy. Homeworks are immense and took me 1-3 days to finish, the techniques and theorems may demand a lot of time to digest.

It is clearly aimed at students without a strong background in informatics or mathematics competitions: if you are just a student who got A- or above in your MATH1014 course, workload will be high due to the homeworks and the trade-off mentioned.

Despite the fact that your background affect your performance significantly, the main purpose of the course is to teach you solve basic mathematical problems applied for computer science. So, since they are not really overloaded with the concepts, the key factor affecting your grade is your accuracy since every single mistake is costly in terms of scores and penalties are generally harsh.

author: Berkin
title: Honors (?) Discrete Mathematical Tools for Computer Science Review
comment_content: This course will cover topics from very diverse fields in a substantial way. Most of the time, you'll only learn about the fundamentals of each topic due to the time constraints and the huge number of topics. Don't be fooled by the COMP in the course code; this course covers MATH subjects that you're going to need for your COMP courses (e.g., modular arithmetic for cryptography and security). The content is fun, but it's not substantially different from 2711's content (apparently, this heavily depends on the instructor). I would've loved to dive deeper into some of the topics. I must admit that I expected much more from an honors course.
comment_teaching: ARYA is clearly passionate about the subjects he teaches. I was familiar with some of the course content beforehand, yet I still attended his lectures solely because of his teaching style. His style can make even the most boring topics enjoyable. He'll emphasize the right spots, engage with the class, and encourage the students to see the bigger picture.
comment_grading:
ARYA has probably one of the best gradings in UST. This year's grading scheme is:
Assignments 20%
Mid-Term Exam 35%
Final Exam 45%

The statistics for the midterm:
Mean: 80.01
Standard deviation: 17.79
Max: 100 (10 students)
Median: 85

The statistics for the final: N/A

For the homework, only some of the problems are graded. They don't specify which problems are going to be graded beforehand, so you need to solve all of the problems for a riskless grade.
comment_workload: Throughout the course, you'll only need to complete 4 assignments. These assignments typically include around 10 questions. Most of the problems are intuitive and approachable, with only a few being quite challenging. I would suggest paying extra attention to homework problems, as ARYA likes to pull questions from the homework and past midterms. You'll get 2 weeks for each assignment, which should be way more than enough.

comment_teaching:
Prof. Arya is passionate in teaching and is willing to answer questions. He always replies to emails quickly. If you can get used to his Indian accent (I did that in 2 weeks), then the teaching would be perfect.
By contrast, tutorial makes me sleepy and the TAs are not that good. Luckily, the content in tutorials are not difficult and they don't take attendance, so skipping the tutorials is not a bad idea.
comment_grading:
Homework: 10%
Midterm: 40%
Final: 50%

It's not hard to get a good mark. The questions in exams are not hard. Many of them are variety of lecture content or homework. Making sure you understand all the content in lectures and finishing all the homework will definitely help you a lot in the exams.
comment_workload: 4 homeworks in total, 2~3h each. You are required to finish each of them in about 2 weeks. Besides, it takes some time to fully understand some difficult content (only in several lectures). Generally speaking, the workload is light for a 4-credit course.

author: PearlLHF
comment_content:
Basic Discrete Math:
Logic
Mod
Combination and permutation
Expectations and variations
Graph...
comment_teaching:
The professor is nice and the teaching is great. He always shows vivid examples and sometimes interacts with students. The explanation of each problem is quite clear, and it is easy to follow him when doing hws and exams.
Tutorial is useful but actually a little boring compared to lectures.
comment_grading:
Easy exams, good grades.
Large A range.
comment_workload: Just focus in class, take the given questions seriously and fully understand all the problems. You don’t need to spend more time on this class.

author: Otaku
comment_content:
The course provides a gentle introduction to students who are interested in further study in computer science the mathematics which commonly appears in their studies. As suggested by its title, this course revolves around the discrete part of mathematics, namely:

1. Logic
2. Number Theory
3. Counting and permutations
3. Discrete probability
4. Induction and recursion
5. Graph theory

The course enables students to develop their mathematical maturity as the content is rather rigorous and formal, though the instructor also succeeds in telling the motivations and intuitions behind formal definition and proofs.

The instructor does not cover all of the graph theory content in the lecture notes because of time constraint.
comment_teaching:
Despite the instructor's Indian accent, his English is easy to comprehend. His teaching is well structured and can be easily followed. He does not like to teach using slideshows. Instead, he loves to use the whiteboard to write the contents down. Some of the content are not present in the lecture notes, which is the same set of notes used in the non-honor version of COMP2711, so do remember to attend the class and take notes. In the mid-term examination, the professor posted a question on the proof of Fermat's Little Theorem, which of course has been taught but only written on the whiteboard! If one wants to avoid losing easy marks it is advised that you do take notes.

He is really knowledgeable in the subject matter since he is a seasoned veteran in the field of algorithms. He offers insights to problems and concepts which could have been very difficult to grasp for inexperienced learners.
comment_grading: The grading is lenient. The instructor said in class that he will give all As. Despite being outperformed by a quarter of the students, the writer still managed to get an A+. Therefore, at least a quarter (25%!) of students gets A+ in this course.
comment_workload: The workload is light as students are only expected to complete and submit four assignments consisting of around 5-10 questions. The questions are challenging yet approachable and insightful. It is expected that the time one spent on this course is mostly due to revision as the instructor provides a large set of problems for us to practice. He provides incentives for the students to practice those questions by reusing them in the written examinations. (He said this in class)

author: Seeking4u()
comment_content:
0. Basics: Sets and Functions (self-study)
1. Logic and Proofs
2. Combinatorics
3. Discrete Probability
4. Number Theory and Cryptography
5. Induction, Recursion, and Recurrence Relations
6. Graphs (wasn't covered thoroughly due to time constraint)
comment_teaching: One of the best instructors from CSE department. Sunil is not just knowledgeable, he can really explain mathematical concepts in an intuitive and organic manner. He's inspiring and humorous as well. I went to every lecture, overall very enjoyable.
comment_grading:
HW 20%, Midterm 35%, Final 45%
Probably one of the most generous grading scheme among courses offered by CSE department. Admittedly most students taking this course are quite diligent and smart, and this drives up the mean scores easily, but if you can keep up with your peers, it's very likely for you to get A-range.
comment_workload: 4 written assignments (feel free to use LaTeX) in total for this semester. Originally it was supposed to be 5, but it was reduced to 4 in the middle of the semester. For each assignment, we had slightly less than two weeks to solve 10++ problems. Better start early because every assignment will definitely have some difficult questions. Pay attention to basically all examples used by Sunil, they might appear in the midterm/final.

author: MEMENTO
comment_content: The content includes logics, combinatorics, probability, number theory, recursion and graph theory. These contents are very interesting if you like math.
comment_teaching: Arya's lectures were very easy to understand, and he was engaging students into his lecture, which was good. I think the TA was quite smart, and his materials clearly helped with understanding the course materials.
comment_grading: The grading was amazing. I think if you get above mean in  mid-term and final and all the assignments, you will get at least A. If you do better than that, like getting around 0.5-0.6sd above mean, you will get A+. However, note that the mean on the exams and assignments tend to be very high, since this is an honor course.
comment_workload: In my opinion, the workload was quite light. For this semester, you only needed to do 4 assignments, each assignments with around 10-15 questions. If you paid full attention during the lecture and reviewed the lecture notes, the assignments would not feel very difficult. Also, you are given about 2 weeks to finish each assignments, so you do not need to rush in time.

author: 當科
title: Excellent course, heavy workload
comment_content:
To quote from the course homepage: "Discrete mathematics needed for the study of computer science: sets, functions; propositional logic, predicate logic, rules of inference, proof techniques; pigeonhole principle, permutations and combinations, binomial coefficients, inclusion-exclusion principle; probability theory, Bayes' theorem, expectation, variance, random variables, hashing; cryptography and modular arithmetic, Euclid division theorem, multiplicative inverse, divisibility, RSA cryptosystem, Chinese remainder theorem; mathematical induction, strong induction and well-ordering property, recursion, recurrence relations"

We were supposed to learn about graphs too but we didn't have enough time so the professor just gave an introduction to the topic.

The concepts were easy, but the actual applications were difficult and complicated. Very interesting nonetheless.

~90% of exam questions are taken from homework and lecture notes. Although Sunil emphasised that we should understand the content instead of memorising, but at the end it was still a memory contest more than anything.
comment_teaching:
Excellent professor. Clear and humorous teaching. Never seen such a high attendance in a 9am class. Sunil is the kind of professor who makes you want to go and check the class schedule website and see whether or not you can enroll in his other courses in the next semester.

Our TA was Yuchen Mao, who was also excellent. He responds quickly to emails and teaches clearly.
comment_grading:
Good grading, I believe almost everyone got A-range.
Above mean = A+
Mean = A
Below mean = A-
(but just don't exploit Sunil's good grade and get ridiculously low scores, someone in our class didn't attend the finals, and idk what grade he/she got at the end)
comment_workload:
5 written assignments in total, we were given 1-1.5 weeks to finish each.
Assignments were very challenging, quite a few assignments I had to finish by googling the answers.

Don't take too many credits along with this course cause it's quite demanding.

author: Sheetal
comment_content: The content is same as the non honors course, but taught at a different pace. The content is majorly about algorithms, probability and running time.
comment_teaching: I absolutely love how sunil arya teaches! His teaching is organic and has a very good flow i.e. he wont teach something simple and expect you to understand a ver hard concept from that. He explains everything step by step and gives a lot a practice!
comment_grading: He needs you to have at least a B+ in the previous MATH Courses. Absolute grading: most people get A range or a B+.
comment_workload: Heavy! But worth it if you really want to learn all the concepts properly!

author: WilLEE
comment_content:
Very similar to normal version of COMP 2711. Lecture notes are from normal version COMP2711 at 2014 Spring. The course is totally MATH course. Extra topics (graph theory) may discuss during lecture time but no notes, have to attend and jot down by yourself.

Book is not required but may useful for handling assignment problem. Book name: Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications 7th edition, Publisher: McGraw-Hill
comment_teaching:
Lecture will elaborate all terms and theories, and may show you the correct way to solve problem. Teaching style is quite traditional, by writing everything on board instead of PowerPoint, but won't boring and easier to follow. Tutorial will discuss the use of theory taught in lecture to solve problem.

The only drawback is Prof. ARYA spent too much time on early topic, causing no time teach topics behind (some of graph theory are skipped and some of them are concluded only in 1~2 lectures.) But overall, his teaching is good for students from all background to follow, and wanna everyone understand before goes on.
comment_grading:
Very good grade, ~75% or above can reach at least A-.

Grading: 20% for assignment, 30% for mid-term, 50% for final.
Mid-term and final may also cover topics in assignments (may not be discussed in lecture), but exam questions are easier than assignments.
comment_workload:
Total 5 assignments, each assignment consist at least 10 questions, some of them consist 20+ questions. They are extremely hard, some of them require extra knowledge which is not included in lecture to solve it. Assignment will be released 2 weeks before due date.

Assignment count for final grading 20%, and some theory not being discussed in lecture but existed on assignment may also included in exam. Have to work extremely hard for good grade.

author: Lifrag
title: Do it if you are ready
comment_content: The content this course covers goes far beyond simply discrete math. It mentions some important computer-science-related logics such as the mathematics beneath recursion, and some parts of statistics also work well with its topic. The only thing to regret is that the part about graphs is barely discussed in the term for a limited number of class hours. Overall, the content is very helpful and meaningful for computer science students. (But for dear JUPAS students, a prerequisite of M1/M2 does not mean M1/M2 works in this course! ;_;)
comment_teaching: Arya is a super exprienced lecturer able to demonstrate complicated and counter-natural discrete math concepts in a way that's far less boring. I can still recall some jokes he made (though those were very discrete-math-specific and engineering-style jokes so not many laughed in class :D). Most proof and equation are established together with we students step by step, by which it provides a lot of insights. Additionally, it is very possible to see that Arya knows the content very well.
comment_grading: The grading of this course as an HONOR COURSE is a bit harsh: to be more exact, the final A range cut-off is somehow similar to the normal version COMP2711 or even higher this semester, while the paper itself turns out to be of a certain level of difficulty, it is not easy to get a good grade compared with other honor courses. But fair enough, this is caused mainly due to too many discrete math masters taking their place this semester to make it a hard game mode, meaning that the culprit may not be the course itself. For reference, my two exams are like mean+1sd and mean+1.5sd and I missed an assignment, then I got a B+. If you would like challenges, 2711H is good for you; or otherwise, go with COMP2711.
comment_workload: The workload is generally low. The only compulsory things else than exams leave only 4 assignments, each of around 13 questions. Well, half of the questions could be very difficult, but after all with some help you can easily get them done. Major workload comes from brain power consumed during lessons and drawn for revision.

author: Wkliin
title: "I will never see you again"
comment_content:
First of all, Amir will leave ust after this term. So, the review is just for fun.

The course will cover the following material:

Proofs and Reasoning: Propositional Logic, First-order (Predicate) Logic, Quantifiers, Proof by contradiction, Mathematical Induction, the Pigeonhole Principle, the Invariant Principle, the Extremal Principle, ...
Set Theory: ZMC, Peano's Axioms, Dedekind Cuts, Relations, Functions, Ordinals and Cardinals, Russel's Paradox, ...
Enumerative Combinatorics: Rules of Sum and Product, Permutations and Combinations, Inclusion-Exclusion, Catalan and Stirling Numbers, Generating Functions, Recurrence Relations, Double Counting, ...
Probability Theory: Discrete and Countable Probability Spaces, Axiomatic Probability Theory, Measure Functions, Lebesgue Integrals, Conditional Probability, Independence, Random Variables, Bayesian Reasoning, Expectation, Stochastic Processes, Markov Chains, Markov Decision Processes, ...
Number Theory: Divisibility, GCD, LCM, Primes, Congruence, Chinese Remainder Theorem, Theorems of Fermat, Euler and Wilson, Quadratic Reciprocity Law, the RSA and El-Gamal Cryptosystems, ...
Graph Theory: Adjacency, Walks, Paths, Cycles, (Strong) Connectivity, Distance, Trees, Spanning Trees, Bipartite Graphs, Matchings, Shortest Paths, ...
Game Theory: Combinatorial Games, Sprague-Grundy Theorem, Normal Form (Nash) Games, Nash Equilibria, Correlated Equilibria, Infinite Games on Graphs, ...

You see sooooooooooooooo many content. And many topics are about graphs (graph theory, game theory markov chain...). Amir really loves graphs. So you survive if you understand those graphs.

If you are curious about what Amir looks like or how he teaches, search him on YouTube and you'll see his channel with lecture recordings.
comment_teaching:
Fast-paced, clear, and a little bit funny.

He conducts the tutorial himself every week, so in total we have about 40 lectures this term.

But the regular lectures are 9 am, so everyone is sleepy including Amir.

TAs? Oh god damn, I just don't want to say. They are ****.
comment_grading:
Not known yet but I heard from others who took his 3711H said the A range is huge. Absolute grading.

40% hw
30% mid
30% final

Homeworks and midterm are really really hard. But surprisingly, final is easy. Amir even gives a calculating question about modulus. I guess that's the gift he gives us before he leave? Hope everyone gets good grades and survives from his huge workload course.
comment_workload:
Huge.

Lectures are compact and fast-paced. Too many contents to be delivered. He also gives way more supporting materials for us to read, but I never read them.

You also need several days to figure out a single problem in homework. They are difficult and time-consuming.

author: unfortunate
title: Equivalent to F.3 Olympiad level math
comment_content:
Pretty standard discrete mathematics stuff including number theory, combinatorics, probability, expected value, recurrence relation and  graph theory.

Difficulty-wise, the course covers only very basic stuff. For number theory, only basic congruences, Euclidean algorithm, Chinese remainder theorem and Fermat's little theorem are taught. For combinatorics, you need to know how to count with star-and-bars and some common double counting skills. For expected value, probability and graph theory, you only need to know very basic stuff.

People who are trained in HK IMO team should find this course very easy since secondary olympiad math is way more comprehensive and in depth than this course. I'm honestly, a little disappointed in this fact since it's supposed to be an honour course.

BTW, there are enough supplementary exercises for students who wish to practise more, which is a big plus for the course.
comment_teaching: The lectures are great, the instructor is passionate  about its teaching and try to invoke thinking from students. I didn't attend any tutorial so I can't say anything about that.
comment_grading:
Haven't received the grading yet. I'll update about this part later.

Update:
Receiving 94 or above will give A+, which is pretty fair.
comment_workload: The workload is light. Only 4 assignments throughout. Most people got full marks in all assignments.

author: XVYI
title: Nice course with good grade
comment_content:

Prof. Sunil is a decent teacher. You can tell that he is passinate about his topics and would like to engage the class. He is very nice when asnwering any of our questions.
However, he would skip some of the details (such as some proofs) in the lecture and reading the lecture notes would help. His handwriting is sometime hard to recognize.

It is good in my perspective as above mean gets A-.

author: JimmyLeung1998
title: Honors Discrete Mathematical Tools for Computer Science
comment_content:
Discrete mathematics needed for the study of computer science:
1. sets, functions;
2. propositional logic, predicate logic, rules of inference, proof techniques;
3. pigeonhole principle, permutations and combinations, binomial coefficients, inclusion-exclusion principle;
4. probability theory, Bayes' theorem, expectation, variance, random variables, hashing;
5. cryptography and modular arithmetic, Euclid’s division theorem, multiplicative inverse, divisibility, RSA cryptosystem, Chinese remainder theorem;
6. mathematical induction, strong induction and well-ordering property, recursion, recurrence relations;
7. graph representation, isomorphism, connectivity, Euler and Hamiltonian paths, planarity, graph coloring.

(1) is not formally taught in class, basically ur knowledge in thz topic is assumed, meaning that u would hv to do some self-study. Btw it is not difficult, just some terminology.
(2) would be a really conceptual topic, no matter how much or how less u memorised, as long as u can use the logic rule to solve problems is ok. Btw I think thz topic would be a good math foundation for one considering taking 3000-4000 math ug courses.
(3) (4) is just slightly > dse lv core math + m1, with sth new like pigenhole and inclusion-exclusion. Btw thz part also overlaps with some math 2000 lv stat prob course, (2411, 21, 31...), which are also in comp curriculum. I think the cse dept should really re-consider to construct a better course structure...
(5) is about mod operation. Comp 1000lv prog course gives u insight, but this course gives u a more in-depth knowledge. Better take it serious for its wide application in many other Comp theory courses or even courses in diff subj, e.g. math courses like Abstract algebra, Number Theory.
(6) is just an extension of Dse lv m2 version MI. Again, what's inmportant is the idea. The stupid work flow and clumsy format would not really bother u much once u get the whole picture
For (7) I think prof usually doesn't hv too much time to cover it. Just like in thz sem, basically he just introduced some terminology and definitions. But those fancy terms are stll important. What u hv to do to deal with thz chapter is memorising stuffs as much as possible. In thz way u cen prevent any "surprises" in final exam.
comment_teaching:
I think prof’s teaching is quite well, but one may first listen him speaking eng with a quite strimg accent..

One thing worth to mention is, prof has a quite
old-style teaching. For most of the topics, he would like to explain by writing everything on board, but not by using ppt slides or showing his work on projector. Plus he does not really hv a nice handwriting. So one might expect to be really concentrated in the lesson to get the idea.

Another thing is, some notes or ppt slides he provides in advance are just other prof’s work. And he just provide to us for ref, but does not choose to follow them strictly. For some topics like (5) concerning number theory and (7) graph theory he even told us to ignore those slides, but instead search Rosens textbook for the definitions and thms he illustrated in the lesson. So u may really want to get a Rosen e-book just in case. Luckily there are some free online versions ~

And he somehow hates people using smart phone in class, but he just managed to accpet student using computers... So u either use phone in secret or use ur computers in class  for learning or other(?) purposes.

Overall his teaching is good and the course really equip u with solid knowledge of some discrete math tools. But somehow u hv to get used to the prof’s teaching style.
comment_grading:
20% for assignments (4-5 / sem)
35% for mid
55% for final
A bit diff from most of the math courses

As far as I know, most of the people taking thz course would get A range. But i think it is just becoz of their strong academic background...
U know, nds and even some locals are really gd at math and logic...
(Thz might explain why as an average-lv HK-er, I only can get a A- grade...)
(Btw no offense.)

One thing gd is, prof is willing to provide pp for us to prepare for mid and final. He may even send u pp in last several sems, as long as sby request in class. And some of the Qs would really re-appear in ur mid / final paper,
meaning that u would hv less risk relative to other comp courses when dealing with exams.

Overall, hard-working can help for obtaining gd grade, which is good for a comp course.
comment_workload:
For the assignments, he usually delivered it 1-2 weeks before. Like those in C++ class, u may not be able to finish it in last minutes. We are talking abt some REAL stuff here. So just be early planned and better prepared.

One thing good is, prof allows or u may say he encourages u to hand in a printed version of ur hw. Plus he specifies that poor hand-writing in hw might cause a lower marks. So u can actually use MS Word or Latex to finish ur hw, which is good for a bad writer like me.

For the difficulty lv, some of the hws would be as difficult as those math honor courses (1023, 24, 2131,...). But as the course title suggested, it is also a H course, so u just gotta deal with it.

To sum up, u might want to expect to hv a huge workload if u r not really that familiar with math. Even if u r, u still need to work hard to compete with others

author: TO Junheng
title: A clever class does some clever things
comment_content:
The course teaches basic number theories, logics, combinatorics, probabilities, and some graphs. Most of the content is easy to grasp but needs your own understanding and assimilation.

If you are a Mainland student, you may find it much alike "xiao xue ao shu" in a more systematic way.
comment_teaching: Professor Arya is very passionate in teaching, and he is so popular with his students that you hardly find a seat if you come at the class-begin time sharp (That's why I always sit in the last row or directly disappear [crying]). The professor speaks a perfect Indian accent, so it is a good chance for you to improve your Indian English listening.
comment_grading:
Homework 10%
Midterm 40%
Final 50%

The exam questions are straightforward, most of them bearing great resemblance to examples covered in lectures. But getting a high score really requires you to elaborate your proofs and solutions elegantly, since ambiguity and lengthiness may lead to score deduction.  

Lenient grading, getting above mean means A range.

For your information, I got 96 in midterm, 100 in final, and the letter grade is A+.
comment_workload: The workload is small. There are only 4 homework sets and two exams.

author: Evanescence
title: Take This Is Always Right
comment_content:

1. Logic
2. Number Theory
3. Combinatorial Mathematics
4. Induction/Recursion
